dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, your response is for a 62-year-old unconscious male located in Apartment W in [CITY].
Hello, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. You called 911 today-can you tell me what happened here?
He was found like this unresponsive in the kitchen. No one else was present.
The patient doesn't respond to voice or touch, indicating unresponsiveness.
Pupils are very pinpoint and not reactive. Agonal breathing is present.
He is agonal. I've never seen that much breath on someone like that before.
He is localizing to pain stimulus but not following commands. GCS is estimated at 5.
Airway is maintained while upright-will continue in that position.
Agonal respirations are shallow and ineffective. RR is around 12 per minute.
Is he on oxygen yet?
Yep, receiving high-flow O2 via non-rebreather at 25 LPM with a BVM assist.
Radial pulse is present, strong, and rapid at 119 bpm. Skin appears warm and dry.
Pupils were 1-mm, non-reactive initially but became reactive post-naloxone at 20:53.
No visible signs of trauma, rashes, or bruising at this time.
Initial pulse was 119, and it has improved to around 106 now.
Respiratory rate remains at 12. Breathing is shallow but more reactive.
Blood pressure is 161 over 97 initially and now has remained relatively stable.
Initial SpO2 is 100% on 25 LPM O2 with BVM concentrate.
Blood sugar is at 86 mg/dL, measured by glucometer.
EKG shows sinus tach from the beginning and remains unchanged so far.
Administered 2 mg of naloxone intranasally to address likely opioid overdose at 20:39.
Capnography initiated to assess CO2 levels, BVM remains on for backup ventilation.
IV access attempted on 18G right AC vein, but unsuccessful at 20:39.
Set up 4-lead ECG monitoring continuously from 20:41.
Conditions are consistent with opioid overdose; protocol 7 activated at 20:39.
IV access remains a no-go. We've tried once in the extremity vein.
SpO2 dropped slightly to 97% post-interventions.
BP reading is 166 over 91. No major drop but still hypertensive.
Second BP was 166/91, according to the time 21:06.
Transporting directly to [COMPANY_NAME] for hospital treatment and definitive care.
A chair is being used by Fire and EMS supervisor to extricate the patient, who is supine.
Is he in a better condition now?
Narcan has sent him into partial awakening. His breathing and reaction improved.
Hospital received full care handover from EMS, including BVM and SpO2 trends.
All patient care changes and EKG readings passed to the nurse at bedside.
Patient ambulated into the bed themselves at the hospital after initial handoff.
No obstructing issues through stretcher to ambulance, no patient drop incidents.
O2 remains viable at 97 with a small decrease but manageable.
BP shows minimal trending lower but remains stable. Naloxone had a delayed but effective response.
Glucose stable and currently not at concern.
Continuous EKG monitoring remains unchanged at sinus tach.
Ongoing stabilizing period with movement to around 106 bpm pulse.
Spontaneous respiration noted approximately 90 seconds after med administration.
No signs or complaints of pain noted-pain score is 0.
GPS route confirmed clear for arrival at [COMPANY_NAME].
Confirmed direct route to [COMPANY_NAME] for acute care, no detours needed.
Head exam: No fractures or deformity. Neck veins unremarkable. Progress to mental status.
Patient has a history of Primary Hypertension and End Stage Renal Disease.
Also documented history of Anxiety, likely contributing or underlying cause.
Lungs clear bilaterally, no adventitious sounds reported in field notes.
Final airway position confirmed as maintained.
Pulse remains strong. Skin warm and perfusing to the periphery.
Narcan administration effective-response partially sustained prior to arrival at hospital.
Ongoing EKG monitoring, no additional meds at this point.
Overdose protocol confirmed complete, patient en route with no acute change in condition.
Extradited and placed on stretcher without delays.
No complications in scene muster or patient transfer.
GPS route confirmed clear for arrival at [COMPANY_NAME].
BVM and SpO2 values remained stable post-stabilization.
All EMS found anatomical integrity intact-no signs of obstruction or trauma.
Consciousness increased from unresponsive to partially alert following Narcan.
Secured patient for transport, no event post transfer to ambulance.
All EPCR records, vitals, and interventions transmitted to hospital staff.
All transition steps and sign-off logs reviewed and completed.
No information on last food or drink, or exact overdose time.
Continuous vitals remain within acceptable range with marginal trends.
Patient en route to facility with no acute intervention required.
With no signs of distress or vocal response, pain score zero remains.
Head, neck, and spinal exams appear normal per documentation.
Chest and lungs remain stable with no additional complaints or findings.
Physical exam reveals ports, likely for dialysis.
Final EKG still shows no change-sinus tach persists and per protocol.
All transition steps and sign-off logs reviewed and completed.
